summary: This is a brief announcement post directing readers to O'Neil's Bloomberg Opinion column about the problematic nature of crime data. The column argues that marijuana arrests serve as one of the few reliable measures of racial bias in policing because usage rates are similar across races but arrest rates differ dramatically. O'Neil warns that marijuana legalization, while positive, will eliminate this important barometer for measuring systemic bias in the criminal justice system.
key_points: Crime data is fundamentally flawed and unreliable for measuring actual criminal behavior
Marijuana arrests uniquely reveal policing bias because usage is similar across races while arrests are not
Legalization will remove one of the few objective measures we have for detecting discriminatory enforcement
The data we use to build predictive policing algorithms is itself a product of biased systems
money_quotes: Let's Not Forget How Wrong Our Crime Data Are
When marijuana is legalized, we'll lose our only reliable barometer of bias in arrests.
key_insight: Marijuana arrest data serves as a rare window into policing bias precisely because usage rates are equal across races, and losing this metric through legalization means losing one of our few tools for measuring systemic discrimination.
